medications.indication_rationale: An oral antihistamine reduces pruritus to help break the itch-scratch cycle that is driving the excoriation and behavioural distress. Dose taken directly from the source note; the patient is an adult so no paediatric dosing is involved.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 10 mg orally, one dose now then once daily
medications.duration: short course while the excoriation settles; review at GP follow-up

medications.indication_rationale: No established secondary infection at presentation — the patient is afebrile and systemically well with no spreading erythema, warmth, swelling, purulence beyond acne pustules, or lymphangitic streaking. Empirical systemic antibiotics are not indicated and would be antibiotic overuse; reserve them for signs of impetiginisation/cellulitis, which are explicitly safety-netted.

Corticosteroids are not a treatment for excoriated acne and can worsen acne; a potent topical steroid on facial skin risks atrophy and steroid-induced acneiform eruption. Not appropriate for this presentation.

The acute problem is excoriation and behavioural distress, not the moment to start long-term acne pharmacotherapy. Isotretinoin requires specialist initiation, pregnancy-prevention and monitoring frameworks and is never started in an acute telehealth contact; the underlying acne is planned with the GP once the raw skin settles.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Support staff benefit from clear instruction on simple daily wound care of the excoriated lesions (gentle cleansing, barrier dressings) — mirrors the source plan of educating support staff on daily wound care.
Behavioural support for the skin-picking/agitation may be valuable longer term via the patient's existing disability care team, but is not an acute telehealth action tonight.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Support worker to gently clean the raw, scratched spots (cooled boiled water or saline) and cover the weeping/bleeding ones with a hydrocolloid patch or clean non-stick dressing as a protective barrier.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: A barrier dressing protects the excoriated skin, promotes healing, reduces the urge and ability to pick, and lowers the risk of secondary infection.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Fit soft cotton gloves at night to physically prevent scratching and let the raw areas heal.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Mechanically interrupts the itch-scratch cycle overnight and protects the skin from fingernails.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use a gentle, non-soap cleanser twice daily and avoid scrubbing, squeezing or picking the spots.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces further skin trauma and irritation while the underlying acne is managed longer term.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Support worker to give the oral antihistamine each evening as advised to control the itch.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Consistent pruritus control breaks the itch-scratch cycle so the skin can heal and the person can sleep.

patient_education_points.point: The anti-itch tablet reduces the deep itch so he can stop scratching and settle to sleep.
The dressings/patches protect the raw spots, help them heal, and make it harder for him to pick at them.
Because the skin is broken and bleeding, it could become infected — watch for spreading redness, heat, swelling, pus, or a fever.
Leave the patches on until they fall off naturally, and use the soft gloves at night.
The underlying skin condition needs a proper plan with the GP once the raw spots have settled.

follow_up_plan.interval: GP review in 3–5 days (sooner if any escalation trigger appears)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Healing of the excoriated lesions, any signs of secondary infection, the behavioural distress and picking, and a longer-term plan for the underlying acne.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Spreading redness, warmth or swelling, pus, fever or feeling unwell — same-day/urgent review; behavioural agitation or self-harm that support staff cannot manage — present to ED, or call 000 if there is immediate danger.

must_include_items: Recognise excoriated, bleeding inflammatory skin lesions and the secondary-infection risk of broken skin
Direct the support worker in simple wound care — gentle cleansing and a protective barrier (e.g. hydrocolloid) dressing over the raw spots
Recommend an OTC oral antihistamine to reduce the itch and break the itch-scratch cycle
Advise protective measures — soft gloves at night and avoiding picking/squeezing
Arrange routine GP review within a few days and give clear infection and behavioural safety-netting
acceptable_alternatives: Any clean non-stick or barrier dressing in place of a hydrocolloid patch
An alternative non-drowsy OTC antihistamine (e.g. loratadine, fexofenadine) instead of cetirizine
A plain gentle emollient/cleanser rather than a specific brand
errors_of_omission: No wound-care advice for the bleeding excoriations
Failing to address the pruritus that is driving the scratching
errors_of_commission: Escalating an afebrile, systemically-well patient to ED or calling an ambulance for excoriated acne with no infection signs
Prescribing systemic antibiotics with no established infection
Recommending a potent topical or oral corticosteroid
Starting isotretinoin or long-term acne therapy acutely via telehealth
Advising the carer to squeeze or de-roof the lesions
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires carer-directed wound care + pruritus management + secondary-infection safety-netting + routine GP follow-up. Over-triage to ED/000, or systemic antibiotics without infection signs, are scored defects. This is NOT an emergency — do not require emergency escalation for a pass.
